ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for fried carrot cake (lo bak go). Mapped to the nearest parent class 'fried food product' (FOODON_03315671) as a defensible approximate anchor. A more specific term for rice-flour-based fried dim sum cakes is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fried snacks — rice-flour-based savoury cake, deep-fried; mapped under FoodEx2 group 'Fried snacks and similar products'
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for lo bak go / fried carrot cake. A0FJP (fried snacks) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried carrot cake or lo bak go as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dim sum serving of fried carrot cake is one piece weighing approximately 60 g, as commonly served in Singapore dim sum restaurants and hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Carrot Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fried carrot cake (plain, deep-fried). All core macros and available micronutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice-flour-based steamed and fried cakes — analogous estimate based on lo bak go / rice flour savoury cake entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for fried carrot cake (lo bak go, deep-fried) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI value of 39 (low) is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: rice-flour-based savoury steamed cake, which has a reported GI in the low range (approximately 35–45) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The fried preparation is expected to have a similar or marginally higher GI due to the Maillard reaction and fat coating slowing gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (39 × 10.68) / 100 ≈ 4.2, using a 60 g serving with 17.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Input record GI confidence was 0.346, reflecting the analogous-estimate basis. This estimate is retained and documented accordingly.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 435 mg per 100 g with negligible potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Fried carrot cake contains 435 mg sodium per 100 g — approaching 30% of the 1500 mg daily sodium target for hypertensive individuals in a single 60 g serving — and should be consumed sparingly, without additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or XO sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice-flour base with moderate carbohydrate load and low protein and fibre buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI is estimated at 39 (low), the rice-flour base provides 17.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.6 g fibre and 1.6 g protein, offering limited glycaemic buffering; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to one piece and pair with a protein-rich dish to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and adds fat; high sodium unfavourable for cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process increases fat content and generates AGEs that promote vascular inflammation, while the 435 mg sodium per 100 g contributes to elevated blood pressure risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer the steamed version (lo bak go) and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: AGE formation from deep-frying and sodium exceeding 400 mg per 100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation, and sodium at 435 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg per 100 g threshold relevant to RA-related tissue swelling; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request the steamed version and avoid dried-shrimp variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried carrot cake (dim sum), known in Cantonese as lo bak go (蘿蔔糕) and in Mandarin as luobo gao (萝卜糕), is a traditional Cantonese dim sum item widely consumed in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants. Despite its English name, it contains no orange carrot; the 'carrot' refers to white radish (daikon / lo bak in Cantonese), which is grated and mixed with rice flour, water, and seasoning to form a steamed savoury cake that is then sliced and pan-fried or deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft and slightly chewy inside. The dish has a mild, savoury, slightly starchy flavour with a pleasant contrast between the crispy fried exterior and the tender rice-flour interior. It is typically served as a dim sum snack or breakfast item, sometimes accompanied by chilli sauce, soy sauce, or XO sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 98 kcal, 17.8 g carbohydrate, 2.3 g fat, 1.6 g protein, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 435 mg sodium. The high sodium content (435 mg per 100 g) is a concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The rice-flour base has a low estimated GI of approximately 39, but the low fibre and protein content provide limited glycaemic buffering for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making the steamed version preferable for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Data sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (confidence 0.95 per input record), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (confidence 0.346 per input), documented accordingly. All multilingual names are verified standard Cantonese/Mandarin, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi renderings.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional fried rice-flour dim sum preparation are not reported in the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry consulted. USDA FoodData Central does not contain a direct equivalent entry for lo bak go / fried carrot cake. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.5 g per 100 g based on the total fat of 2.3 g and typical vegetable-oil frying fat profile for rice-flour snacks; sugar_g estimated at 0.5 g per 100 g reflecting trace natural sugars from white radish in the absence of a direct HPB sugar value. GI value of 39 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest rice-flour savoury cake entry; original input GI confidence was 0.346. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (39 × 10.68) / 100 ≈ 4.2 for a 60 g serving. The food_subgroup 'Local ethnic snacks' and food_group 'Snacks' are preserved from the canonical HPB input. Dietary flags: vegetarian and vegan status assumes the plain version without dried shrimp; halal status assumes no pork lard used in frying (plain version). FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are approximate anchors — no exact codes exist for this specific dish in current ontology releases.
